entry.title: Re: SAP POs to Ariba B&I without Commerce Automation
entry.content:
Hi @velayudham 
This is the standard when you have B&I.
Here you can have the data flow of the CIG integration: https://help.sap.com/docs/sisgw/sap-ariba-cloud-integration-gateway-overview-guide/sap-ariba-buying-and-invoicing?locale=en-US&version=LATEST 

By default:

  1. Requisitionner creates a PR and submit it for approval,  after submition, PR can be created in S4 with the Real Time budget Check (optional).
  2. When the PR is approved in B&I, another budget check happens to make sure the budget is still avalable
  3. Then a PO is created in Ariba B&I, the the PO is sent to S4 t, and the S4 PO number is send back to Ariba B&I 
  4. Afterward, the Carbon Copy of the PO is sent directly to Supplier on SAP SBN, 
  5. Supplier can send Order Confimation to Ariba B&I (not sent to S4)
  6. Supplier can send Ship Notice to B&I (not sent to S4)
  7. Upon the goods reception, the requisitionner creates a Goods Receipt which is then sent and posted in S4
  8. The Supplier on SBN send the Invoice to B&I
  9. Invoice Reconcisliation happens in B&I and 
  10. Then invoice is posted in S4
  11. Buyer pays the Supplier to finalise the process.

For those who have taken it recently, could you please share your experience?

I’m especially interested in:

Any feedback or tips would be greatly appreciated 
Thanks in advance for your help!

entry.title: Re: Workaround to confirm a Combined Order operation with...
entry.content: @Dominik_Tylczynski as you know I'm not very technical.  If we use a Confirmation BAdI to change a CO19 confirmation into a CO11N confirmation, don't we need an object that will accept the "Start Processing" time event Confirmation and then the "Finish Processing" time event confirmation? It cannot be the Header Combined Order since it will error.  What object will receive these 2 time-event confirmations?  The information for the time ticket confirmation (elapsed time and yield) CO11N will be known only after both time event confirmations have been performed.

entry.title: ABAP AI Podcast: Reflecting on 2025 and Looking Ahead to 2026
entry.content:
2025: The Year ABAP AI Became Tangible

As Simona and I reflected on 2025, one theme stood out: it was the year ABAP AI stopped being a promise and became something developers could touch, use, and trust. That shift didn’t happen all at once — it was shaped across the year through a series of releases that progressively deepened what ABAP and AI could do together.

The journey began with Release 2502, our first big delivery, which marked a turning point for the ABAP developer experience. This release introduced the foundational AI capabilities that developers immediately felt in their workflow: the initial wave of explain ability for ABAP and CDS, predictive and context‑aware code suggestions, and early ABAP Cloud compliance insights. 2502 was a statement: ABAP AI had arrived, and it was ready for real use cases.

Building on this momentum, Release 2505 expanded and strengthened the story. the RAP BO Generator for Fiori applications. The explain model grew to cover more ABAP constructs, predictive assistance became more capable, and clean‑core compliance checks were enhanced to give developers clearer, more actionable guidance.

Toward the end of the year, Release 2508 brought refinement and specialization. AI guidance became more scenario‑aware — better tuned for migration, modernization, and, AI‑supported extensibility patterns. Autogenerated documentation became richer and more reliable. Custom code migration also received a boost with deeper AI‑powered insights and more intelligent documentation support — small but meaningful improvements that helped teams move faster and with more confidence.

And throughout all of this, we made sure customers in private cloud were not left behind. With the 2025 PCE add‑ins, Joule for Developers became available to S/4HANA Private Cloud Edition customers as well — enabling AI‑assisted custom code migration, modernization support, and clean‑core guidance even for those on older releases. This was a major step in democratizing ABAP AI and bringing its value to a much broader installed base.

Key Features and Developer Impact

With Joule for Developers, ABAP developers immediately benefited from a range of intelligent capabilities, including:

  • Predictive and context‑aware code suggestions
  • Automated ABAP Cloud compliance checks
  • Natural‑language explanations for both legacy and new ABAP code and extended explain use for autogenerated technical documentation
  • Developers could even generate complete Fiori applications simply by describing the functional requirements.

These advancements significantly accelerated development cycles and reduced manual effort. AI also enhanced custom code migration by proposing simplifications, improving code quality, and easing the transition to clean‑core architectures.

ABAP and AI: A Strengthened Relationship

Simona shared insights into why ABAP required SAP‑specific AI models. Because ABAP is proprietary, large language models were not initially trained on it. SAP addressed this by training internal models on over 250 million lines of ABAP code and 30 million lines of CDS views, unlocking high‑quality AI support tailored specifically to ABAP. 

To hear the full conversation, listen to the episode: ABAP AI Podcast: Reflecting on 2025 and Looking Ahead to 2026 

Another important point we touched on in the conversation is how ABAP AI brings together the best of both worlds. SAP pairs its own ABAP‑specialized models—trained on hundreds of millions of lines of ABAP and CDS code—with best‑of‑breed third‑party foundation models. This hybrid approach ensures developers always get the most accurate and context‑aware support for ABAP tasks, while still benefiting from the broad language understanding and creativity of large general‑purpose models. And for customers who are primarily interested in explain ability, SAP also offers the explain‑only model through the AI Hub, making ABAP‑focused code explanations accessible even without the full Joule for Developers experience.

Far from replacing developers, AI strengthens the ABAP ecosystem. The ABAP community has shown excitement and readiness to embrace these advancements, seeing AI as a powerful augmentation of their skills.                                  

2026: Scaling the Promise

Looking forward, 2026 is shaping up to be a pivotal year of scaling ABAP AI. SAP plans to bring AI capabilities to customers running older private cloud releases through a side‑by‑side version of Joule for Developer, ensuring broader access also for customers on older S4HANA versions.  

SAP will also add to it agentic tools and frameworks and an ABAP MCP Server, enabling SAP teams and partners to build intelligent agents that automate development tasks and connect seamlessly into an integrated agentic ecosystem. Investment in AI‑assisted custom code migration will continue, focusing on clean‑core compliance and automated code simplification proposals.

VS Code and Modern Tooling

A major priority for 2026 is establishing VS Code as a first‑class development environment for ABAP. By combining VS Code’s modern developer experience with Joule and AI‑centric capabilities, SAP aims to reposition ABAP as a future‑proof, cloud‑native, enterprise‑grade language.

Trust and Enterprise Readiness

As AI evolves, SAP continues to emphasize safety, compliance, and enterprise‑grade ethics. All AI use cases undergo rigorous validation to meet SAP’s security and trust standards.

Custom Code Migration (CCM)

A core focus area we highlighted in the podcast is the continued evolution of Custom Code Migration (CCM) and its central role in helping customers transition to clean‑core, cloud‑ready architectures. Throughout 2025, AI became a powerful companion in this journey — analyzing legacy patterns, identifying simplification opportunities, and guiding developers toward more future‑proof designs. With Joule for Developers, teams benefit from usage‑aware insights, automated refactoring suggestions, and richer technical documentation that dramatically reduces the manual effort usually tied to migration projects. As we move into 2026, CCM is set to scale even further, with deeper automation and more intelligent guidance that make modernization and S/4HANA transitions faster, more predictable, and far less error‑prone.

Conclusion

The year 2025 proved the real, tangible value of ABAP AI. In 2026, ABAP and AI will become inseparable—empowering developers with intelligent tools that make coding faster, smarter, and more reliable. The vision is clear: the future of ABAP development is deeply AI‑enhanced, enabling organizations to innovate with confidence and speed.

entry.title: Re: Workaround to confirm a Combined Order operation with...
entry.content:
Hi @ann_hustis I suggested to use BAPIs (SAP provided interfaces) not BAdIs (enhancement points). In my opinion there is no way to rework CO19 to post a time ticket.

What can be done is to implement a custom transaction that would get the same data as CO19. It would store a "start" event in a custom DB table. Then when a "end" event is entered in the transaction, it could post a time ticket with the BAPI. So users could use the custom transaction in pretty much the same way as CO19, but finally the confirmation would be posted as a time ticket.

entry.title: Exploring Enhanced Mass Actions and Compensation Forms in Job Scheduler
entry.content:
In this blog post, I’ll walk you through the enhanced capabilities of the Job Scheduler feature, with a special focus on Mass Actions and Compensation Forms for templates. I’ll also share a few tested scenarios to demonstrate how these enhancements work in real-world use cases, making job scheduling more efficient and flexible than ever.

About Mass Action:

Mass Action in Executive Review now supports additional tabs, fields, and enhanced functionality. With this update, Mass Action can be applied across the Salary, Bonus, and Stock tabs. In addition to numeric values, you can now apply Mass Actions using percentage values, providing greater flexibility when managing compensation changes.

Field Eligibility and Considerations
The following conditions and field types determine whether a field can be updated using Mass Action:
• Standard fields must be editable, included in the template, and users must have field-based write permissions.
• Supported standard field IDs include:
• Salary tab (previously supported): merit, promo, extra, extra2, lumpsum, lumpsum2
• Bonus tab: bonusAdjustment
• Stock tab: stock, stockUnits, options, stockOther1, stockOther2, stockOther3
• Custom fields that are editable, reportable, and have appropriate field-based write permissions are supported.
• Fields with dropdown (enum) selections are supported.
• Money and amount custom fields that support totals are supported.
• For percent-type fields, only percentage-based changes are allowed.
• Money and Amount fields can be updated either by a percentage or a fixed amount.
• String fields display a text input when using Mass Action.
• Dropdown fields display available values for selection during Mass Action.
• By default, only fields with existing values are updated by Mass Action. However, String and enum fields are updated even if their values are blank or null.

These enhancements significantly expand the applicability of Mass Action, enabling support for a wider range of fields and business scenarios.

How It looks Now:

In the current version, the Mass Action functionality is available for additional standard fields on the stock and bonus compensation worksheet tabs and can also be applied to custom fields on any tab meeting certain criteria. Also, you can apply modifications to the entered values in percentage terms when relevant.

 

How It looks Before:

In the previous version, Mass Action was only available for select standard fields on the Salary tab. Also, you could not make the modifications to the Program in percent values for relevant fields.

 

 

Granting Permissions for the Mass Action Feature

Before using the Mass Action feature, ensure that the appropriate permissions are granted to users.
Navigation Path:
Manage Permission Roles → Compensation and Variable Pay → Mass Action – Compensation

 

Testing Mass Action Scenarios in Executive Review

The validation of the Mass Action feature in Executive Review by testing cost increase scenarios across different programs. Each scenario documents the end-to-end process used to apply the change and verify the results through reporting.


Scenario 1: Increasing the “Options” Program Cost by 2%

In this scenario, the Options program cost was increased by 2% using the Mass Action functionality. The following steps were performed to execute and validate the update:

  1. Navigate to Executive Review.
  2. Select the current planning template.
  3. Click the Mass Action button from the worksheet toolbar.
  4. The Mass Action pop-up window opens.
  5. From the Programs drop-down menu, select Options.
  6. From the Action drop-down menu, select Increase.
  7. In the Modify the total program cost by section:
  • Enter the percentage increase (for example, 2).
  • Select the % checkbox.
  • Click Apply.

8. A confirmation pop-up appears. Click OK to proceed.

9. Once the job is successfully submitted, click OK again to close the confirmation message.

10. From the module picker drop-down, navigate to Reporting and select View Schedules.

11. Verify that the job status is marked as Completed.

12. Under the Action section, download the report.

13. Review the report to validate the Before and After cost changes for the Options program.

Please refer to the screenshots below for visual guidance and confirmation of the steps.

 

 

Scenario 2: Increasing the “Stock (RSUs)” Program Cost by 5%

This scenario validates a 5% increase to the Stock (RSUs) program using the same Mass Action workflow. The steps followed are outlined below:

  1. Navigate to Executive Review.
  2. Select the current planning template.
  3. Click the Mass Action button from the worksheet toolbar.
  4. The Mass Action pop-up window opens.
  5. From the Programs drop-down menu, select RSUs.
  6. From the Action drop-down menu, select Increase.
  7. In the Modify the total program cost by section:
  • Enter the percentage increase (for example, 5).
  • Select the % checkbox.
  • Click Apply.

8. A confirmation pop-up appears. Click OK to proceed.

9. Once the job is successfully submitted, click OK again to close the confirmation message.

10. From the module picker drop-down, navigate to Reporting and select View Schedules.

11. Confirm that the job status is Completed.

12. Under the Action section, download the report.

13. Review the report to validate the Before and After cost changes for the RSUs program.

Screenshots are provided below to support and visually confirm the execution and validation steps.

 

 

 

Update Compensation Forms for Template – Scheduled Job


What Is It?
The Update Compensation Forms for Template job can now be scheduled and executed directly from the Scheduled Job Manager in Admin Tools. This enhancement enables administrators to run the job on demand or schedule it for later, without requiring support intervention.


Why Is It Important?
With this update, you can configure and monitor the job using the Job Scheduler and Job Monitor for compensation worksheets. The job uses the same parameters as the Update All Worksheets tool, ensuring consistent results while providing greater flexibility and control.

Configuration Requirement
As this is a customer-configured upgrade, to enable the feature:
1. Navigate to Scheduled Job Manager → Job Scheduler → Create Job Request
2. Enable the option to create a job of type Update Compensation Forms for Template

Role-Based Permission Prerequisites
To configure and run this job, the following permissions are required:
• Administrator Permissions → Admin Center Permissions → Manage Scheduled Jobs
• Administrator Permissions → Admin Center Permissions → Monitor Scheduled Jobs
• Administrator Permissions → Manage Compensation → Update Compensation Forms for Template.


Scheduling the “Update Compensation Forms” Job from the Frontend

After the H225 release, I had the opportunity to schedule the Update Compensation Forms job directly from the frontend, rather than through provisioning. This approach worked as expected and successfully executed the job based on the configured parameters.

Below is the step-by-step process followed to create, schedule, and manage the job

  1. Navigate to Scheduled Job Manager.
  2. Click the Job Scheduler tab.
  3. Click Create Job Request.
  4. Enter the following details:
  5. Job Name – Enter a name for the job.
  6. Job Type – Select Update Compensation Forms for Template.
  7. Job Owner – Select the user recorded as running the job. The owner must have all required permissions. Example: Emily Clark ‎(admin)
  8. Compensation Template – Select the current Compensation planning template.
  9. Job Parameters – Job parameters vary by job type. Select the following checkboxes as required:

I. Add newly eligible employees to the compensation worksheet.

II. Update the worksheet to reflect employee eligibility changes.

ii(a). Reapply defined eligibility rules before updating the worksheet.

10.  Job Occurrence – Define how frequently the job should run.

11. Time Zone – Select a time zone from the dropdown. By default, the time zone is UTC+0000 (UTC).

If Dependent Of is selected for the Occurrence field, the Time Zone field is disabled and inherited from the prerequisite job.

12. Occurrence (Recurring) – Select a recurrence pattern: Daily, Weekly, Monthly, or Yearly.

13. Select the Start and End date and time.

14. Notification Recipients – Enter additional email recipients (comma-separated) to receive job notifications, excluding the job owner.

Send Email When Job Fails – Select this option to send an email notification if the job fails.

Send Email When Job Completes – Select this option to send an email notification when the job completes successfully.

15. Click Submit to save and schedule the job to run at the specified time. OR

16. Click Save to save the job request without scheduling it.

Note: This job can be stopped in two ways:

  1. Specify an End Date during job scheduling. The job will automatically stop on the specified date.
  2. To stop the job manually, follow the steps below:
  3. Click the Job Scheduler tab.
  4. In the Job Type section, select the job type (for example, Update Compensation Forms for Template) and click Go.
  5. Select the appropriate job from the list, click Actions, and then click Cancel.

 

 

Note: All screenshots shown are from the Salesdemo instance.

entry.title: SAP Success Plans - Der neue Support-Ansatz von SAP für die Cloud
entry.content:
SAP führt im März 2026 ein neues Support-Modell ein, das die Zusammenarbeit mit Kunden vereinfachen soll. In einer Session im Rahmen der IT Online Konferenz erklärten Dr. Stephanie Lockemann (Head of Premium Engagements and Customer Innovation Services) und Simone Ruprechter (Head of Solution Sales Business Development and Go-to-Market Services) die Neuerungen in einem Interview. Anbei eine Zusammenfassung.

Warum werden die Success Pläne als ein neues Support-Modell benötigt?

Veränderte Marktbedingungen

Die Rahmenbedingungen für Unternehmen haben sich gewandelt. Märkte folgen nicht mehr vertrauten Mustern, sondern verändern sich in kürzeren Zyklen. Wachstumsphasen, Inflation und Rezession wechseln sich schneller ab. Lieferketten geraten ins Stocken, Fachkräfte fehlen, und geopolitische Entwicklungen ändern sich im Tagesrhythmus.

Gleichzeitig entstehen neue Chancen: Daten liefern Erkenntnisse in Echtzeit, künstliche Intelligenz steigert die Produktivität, und neue Geschäftsmodelle werden möglich. Entscheidend ist nicht mehr die Unternehmensgröße, sondern Geschwindigkeit, Transparenz und Anpassungsfähigkeit.

Kundenfeedback als Treiber

Neben den Marktbedingungen war das Kundenfeedback zentral für die Neuausrichtung. Kunden erwarten SAP in einer beratenden Rolle über den gesamten Lebenszyklus ihrer Lösung - nicht nur bei der Implementierung. Sie brauchen vorausschauende Guidance, Unterstützung bei neuen Releases und kontinuierliche Beratung, um den Return on Investment zu maximieren und TCO zu senken.

Die drei SAP Success Pläne im Überblick

Das neue Modell besteht aus drei aufeinander aufbauenden Success Plans:

Foundational Success Plan

Der Foundation Success Plan erweitert das bestehende SAP Enterprise Support Angebot und richtet sich an Kunden mit standardnahen Systemen und geringeren Support-Anforderungen Alle SAP‑Cloud-Kunden erhalten ihn automatisch. Er bietet:

  • 24/7 Support entlang Service Level Agreements
  • Managed Services für den Cloud-Betrieb
  • Self-Service-Inhalte für Onboarding und Launch
  • Begleitung bei der Transformation mit SAP Cloud ALM
  • Learning Journeys für Schulungen

Advanced Success Plan

Der Advanced Plan baut darauf auf und löst Preferred Success sowie Preferred Success Expanded Edition ab. Er bietet u.a,:

  • Verbesserte Service Level Agreements
  • Innovation and Adoption Guidance für neue Releases und Features
  • KI-basiertes Systemmonitoring mit automatisierten Empfehlungen
  • Activation and Optimization Services (Microservices mit 8-16 Stunden)
  • Einen remote Success Expert, der einen Serviceplan erstellt
  • Kunden können alle Entitlements unbegrenzt nutzen

Max Success Plan

Der Max Plan ermöglicht eine strategische Partnerschaft mit SAP. Er kombiniert alle Leistungen aus Foundational und Advanced und ergänzt sie um:

  • Ein dedizierter Success Plan Manager
  • Zugriff auf das Premium Engagement Service Portfolio
  • Services für Business Process Improvement, Complex Transformation und Safeguarding
  • Maßgeschneiderte, individuelle Services incl. Prototyping
  • Enhanced Case Management mit dediziertem Ansprechpartner
  • Vor-Ort-Unterstützung bei Bedarf

Der Max Plan integriert das bisherige Premium Engagement Services Angebot.

Was sind Praxisbeispiele der SAP Success Pläne?

Business AI Nutzung

Bei Business AI sieht es wie folgt aus:

Foundational: Zugriff auf Lösungsdokumentation über help.sap.com, Learning Journeys, Aktivierung von Joule über Self-Services

Advanced: Zusätzliche Leistungen zur Identifikation des Business AI Potenzials, strategische Aktivierung von Anwendungsfällen, fortlaufende Produktberatung, Guidance und Optimierungsempfehlungen

Max: Definition der KI-Strategie, Erstellung einer Daten- und Architektur-Roadmap, Entwicklung von Prototypen für maßgeschneiderte KI-Anwendungsfälle

Business Data Cloud (BDC) - Neues Release

Ein konkretes Szenario bei einem neuen BDC-Release zeigt die Flexibilität durch die Kombination verschiedener Layer:

Foundation: Zugriff auf Lösungsdokumentation über help.sap.com, neue Lerninhalte über Learning Journeys 
Advanced:
  • Release Guidance für Überblick über neue Funktionen
  • Key Feature Advisory für interessante Features
  • Best Practice Advisory für Einsatzszenarien
  • Aktivierung des Features zum Testen
  • Transformation Advisory für die Implementierungs-Roadmap
  • Kontinuierliches KPI-Monitoring und Optimierungsvorschläge
Max:
  • Proof of Concept bei großflächigem Rollout
  • Begleitung der Implementierung
  • Safeguarding der Implementierung

Was sind Kernprinzipien der SAP Success Pläne?

Design Prinzipien sind die Folgenden

  • Umfassendes, maßgeschneidertes Serviceportfolio für alle Phasen
  • Einheitliches Front Office und eine Governance
  • Ein Ansprechpartner über alle Lösungen hinweg
  • End-to-End Betrachtung der Lösungslandschaft
  • Individueller Serviceplan je nach Situation

Welche Vorteile bieten die SAP Success Pläne?

  • Flexibilität: Unlimitierter Abruf von Entitlements ermöglicht schnelles Ausprobieren neuer Features ohne Approval-Prozesse oder separate Angebote
  • Agilität: Schnellere Innovation durch unkomplizierte Service-Nutzung aus verschiedenen Ebenen
  • Integrated Delivery: Ein einheitliches Governance‑Modell sorgt für eine optimale Lieferung - unabhängig davon, aus welchem Level oder Portfolioelement die Services kommen
  • Individualisierung: Lösungsspezifische Wahl des passenden Plans - verschiedene SAP-Systeme können unterschiedliche Success Plans nutzen
  • Ressourceneffizienz: Einsparung interner Ressourcen und externer Beratung durch SAP-Services

Fragen und Antworten zu den SAP Success Plänen

Für wen ist welcher Plan geeignet?
Die Success Plans eignen sich für alle Kundengruppen. Durch die drei aufeinander aufbauenden Ebenen findet jeder Kunde das passende Modell - abhängig von Komplexität, Bedarf und strategischer Bedeutung der jeweiligen Lösung.

Wie ist die Verfügbarkeit? Launch-Termin: Das neue Support-Modell startet am 3. März 2026.

Wie ist der Übergang von bestehenden Verträgen? 
Laufende Premium Engagement und Preferred Success Verträge bleiben bestehen und laufen über die gesamte Vertragslaufzeit. Nach Vertragsende erfolgt die Überführung in das neue Modell gemeinsam mit dem Kunden. Ein früherer Wechsel ist nach Absprache mit den bekannten Ansprechpartnern möglich.

Gilt das neue Modell nur für Cloud-Kunden? Ja, die Success Plans sind für Cloud-Lösungen konzipiert. On-Premise-Kunden können weiterhin Premium Engagement Services in einem revidierten Modell nutzen.

Was passiert mit laufenden Preferred Success oder Premium Engagement Verträgen? Bestehende Verträge laufen über die vereinbarte Vertragslaufzeit. Die Überführung ins neue Modell erfolgt nach Vertragsende oder bei Bedarf früher in Abstimmung mit dem Kunden.

Kann man verschiedene Success Plans für unterschiedliche Lösungen wählen? Ja, Kunden haben volle Flexibilität. Für jede SAP-Lösung kann der passende Plan gewählt werden. Der Max Success Plan stellt dabei die End-to-End-Beratung über alle Lösungen sicher.

Wann sind Kosten und Preise verfügbar? Aussagen dazu sind ab März 2026 möglich.

Für welche Kundengruppen sind die Success Plans geeignet? Für alle Kundengruppen. Die drei aufeinander aufbauenden Level bieten für jeden Bedarf das passende Modell.

Wo finden sich weiterführende Informationen? Nach dem Launch im März folgt eine dreiteilige Webcast-Reihe in Deutsch und Englisch mit den Schwerpunkten: Überblick über die Success Plans / Deep Dive Advanced Success Plan / Deep Dive Max Success Plan. Die Anmeldung ist bereits hier möglich.

Was sind Tipps für die Praxis? Hierzu wurden folgende Punkte genannt:

  1. Frühzeitig klassifizieren: Bewerten Sie Ihre SAP-Lösungen nach strategischer Bedeutung, Komplexität und Standardnähe, um den passenden Plan zu identifizieren.
  2. Ansprechpartner kontaktieren: Nutzen Sie bestehende SAP-Kontakte für eine individuelle Bewertung und Empfehlung.
  3. Flexibilität nutzen: Denken Sie lösungsspezifisch - nicht jede Lösung benötigt denselben Support-Level.
  4. Entitlements ausschöpfen: Im Advanced und Max Plan sind Services unlimitiert abrufbar. Nutzen Sie diese Flexibilität für schnelle Innovation.
  5. End-to-End betrachten: Lassen Sie sich vom Success Plan Manager oder Expert einen ganzheitlichen Serviceplan erstellen.
  6. Learning Journeys einplanen: Nutzen Sie die integrierten Schulungsangebote für eine erfolgreiche Adoption.
  7. Webcast-Reihe besuchen: Die geplanten Deep Dives bieten detaillierte Einblicke in Advanced und Max Plans.

Zusammenfassung

SAP reagiert mit den neuen Success Plans auf veränderte Marktbedingungen und Kundenbedürfnisse. Das dreistufige Modell - Foundational, Advanced und Max - bietet von Self-Service bis zur strategischen Partnerschaft das passende Engagement für jeden Bedarf. 

Kernelemente sind Flexibilität, integrierte Delivery mit einer Governance und die Möglichkeit, für unterschiedliche Lösungen verschiedene Pläne zu wählen. Bestehende Verträge bleiben selbstverständlich weiter gültig. Die Success Plans bündeln SAPs Cloud‑Support‑Kompetenz in einem klar strukturierten Modell, das Stabilität, Innovationstempo und strategische Beratung vereint.

So begleitet SAP Unternehmen durch den gesamten Lebenszyklus ihrer Softwarelösungen. Mehr Infos gibt es hier in den Webinaren.

entry.title: Capitalization Key - A hidden gem in IM-AA integration to differentiate asset capitalization values
entry.content:
Have you ever wondered, how can I post different settlement values across multiple depreciation areas while using AuCs (Assets Under Construction) ? Well, in this short article I will show you a functionality in SAP that has not been broadly covered and that a lot of consultants don't know about.

Let's take the following example: under IAS38, certain eligible intangible assets should be recognized as costs in IFRS. In the other hand, some local GAAP (such as France GAAP) require that these assets stay in the balance sheet. In this case, the same costs are to be settled differently between IFRS (not capitalized) and FRGAAP (Capitalized) books. And this is where the Capitalization Key comes in.

What's a Capitalization Key ?

The Capitalization Key specifies that certain cost elements in certain depreciation areas of the asset under construction are not capitalized (either wholly or in part). While configuring the key, you can specify (among other criteria) :

  • The capitalisation version, which corresponds to the depreciation area(s) (configured in transaction OKGL)
  • The cost element
  • The percentage of capitalization

Transaction OKGK

Portions of costs that are not capitalized are periodically cleared as non-operating expense. The system transfers the acquisition and production costs, which do require capitalization, to the asset under construction.

All Capitalization Key-related configuration steps are available in the following path:

SPRO > Investment Management > Projects as Investment Measures > Capitalization Values per Depreciation Area

 

How does it work in a business process ?

Once the AuC is automatically created via WBS (we're using here WBS as investment measures with Asset Accounting integration), it is mandatory to maintain the capitalization key in the AuC's master data. It is also possible to default the value capitalization key for a certain asset class if necessary.

  1. The WBS collects the costs
  2. At the time of settlement to the AuC, the program will detect if a capitalization key is used
  3. The settlement is done following the configuration specified in Transaction OKGK

 

Disclaimer

  • The costs that are not capitalized are cleared as non-operating expenses. It should be noted that only one account can be specified in configuration for clearing. Some business cases require to keep the original cost account instead of the non-operating clearing account, this can only achieved via an enhancement.
  • The ability to distinguish which costs should be capitalized is only available when using Investment Management (IM) objects — such as an internal order or a WBS element. Capitalization performed purely through FI‑AA transactions is not supported.

entry.title: RAP‑Based Service Does Not Support Aggregation Totals with Filters and Group‑By
entry.content:
Problem Description:

We are implementing a Fiori Elements List Report using a RAP‑based OData service (OData V2 or V4).
The business requirement is to show aggregated totals (SUM) for multiple numeric columns. These totals must update dynamically whenever:

  • Filters are applied
  • Group By is enabled
  • Group levels expand/collapse
  • Sorting or table personalization is applied

Most Important Requirement:

When the List Report table is grouped, the system must display an aggregation subtotal row for each group (group-level total), not only a grand total.

However, RAP currently does not allow analytical annotations or analytical CDS entities to be used with transactional behavior. Because of this limitation:

  • We cannot enable annotations such as
    • @analytics.query: true
    • @DefaultAggregation: #SUM
    • @Aggregation.default: #SUM
  • RAP services cannot perform backend aggregations or expose group-level totals.
  • As a result, neither grand totals nor group-level totals can appear in the List Report.

Observed Limitation:

Backend (RAP):

  • RAP business objects with behavior definitions do not support analytical CDS views.
  • Aggregations and group-by queries are not supported in RAP OData V2 or OData V4.
  • Analytical annotations cannot be used on projection or interface views participating in RAP.

entry.title: Re: QM: Usage Decision allowed without Results Recording...
entry.content: There is no standard SAP configuration that will hard block a Usage Decision (UD) until results recording is completed. By design, SAP QM only issues a warning (“Inspection completion forced”) but still allows UD posting. If you want to enforce a strict restriction, you need to use a status profile or implement a user exit/BAdI.

entry.title: Custom Fields in SAP S/4 HANA PUBLIC CLOUD
entry.content:
Introduction

In Key User Extensibility,  Custom fields can be added using Custom Fields App.

Custom Fields in SAP S/4 HANA PUBLIC CLOUD

Custom Fields is one of the Key User Extensibility Tools                        

In the Custom Fields App, 

  • Create and edit custom fields (extend the SAP table, application, CDS views and OData services)
  • Delete an existing custom field
  • Manage the usages of a custom field such as other Uls, reports, forms, service interfaces, processes, search
  • Define fields of type Text, number, amount, quantity, time, date, timestamp, ...
  • Value help views based on fixed code list or based on CDS view.

Steps to Create Custom Fields

Navigation to Custom Fields App

Custom Fields App

Click on Create

Choose Required Business Context

Add Label name (Identifier, Tooltip appears automatically based on given label name).

Choose Type of a Custom Fields.

Here Phone Number is chosen, System automatically assigns length of it.

If Text Type is chosen, then manually length should be given.

Based on the length of a field, business context capacity will be increased.

Available Types for the field

Choose Create or Create and Edit options

Choose APIS

To expose custom fields through APIs, ensure the necessary APIs are enabled so the fields become accessible.

To display custom fields in the SAP GUI, make sure the relevant data sources are enabled so the fields appear on the screen

Enable the SAP GUI

To expose custom fields through services, ensure the necessary service are enabled so the fields become accessible.

To make custom fields available in form templates, enable the appropriate templates to ensure the fields are accessible

General Information provides information of the custom fields with all label texts

Different Labels can be maintained for a custom field.

Custom field is added in the Purchase Order Header Screen.

Extended CDS View of Custom Field

After Creating Custom field and enabling related CDS View or API, that CDS View will be extended with that custom field.

For every custom field, separate Extended view will be generated.

Select the Extended view, it shows the custom field.

The custom field is added in the Extended view of Purchase Order CDS View

References

  • Before Getting Started with Custom Fields | SAP Help Portal
  • Creating Custom Fields | SAP Help Portal
  • Key User Extensibility | SAP Help Portal
  • Extensibility | SAP Help Portal
  • Extend and Integrate Your SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition | SAP Help Portal
  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition | SAP Help Portal | SAP Help Portal

Conclusion

Using Custom Fields App,

Custom fields can be added to the UI.

Create and edit custom fields and Delete an existing custom field.

Value help views can be added to the custom fields.

Custom fields can also be added to the custom forms.

entry.title: Re: SAP Discrete manufacturing: Manufacturing date is aut...
entry.content:
• Configuration alone (OPKP) will only change the default proposal, not enforce manual entry.
  • To meet your client’s requirement (manual entry at GR), you need a combination:
    1. Set OPKP to C (GR date) so SAP doesn’t propose system date at order release.
    2. Use batch class characteristic control or a BAdI to enforce manual entry during GR.

entry.title: A.I Assisted Transition at your own Pace: BW7.5 to BDC Pilot Experiences
entry.content:
This blog is part of a series related to a complex BW7.5 customer case where we explored together the opportunity to transition to BDC. You can find the intro blog below, plus the links to the subsequent blogs will be listed here -

  •  Link 1
  • Link 2 
  • Link 3

The Time is Now

At the time of typing this it is still early 2026. The year’s priorities and goals are being set. For many of our BW customers, the end of maintenance deadline set at the end of 2027 for NW 7.5 is looming large. Questions such as the below are being asked –

  • How can we maintain what we have built over many years without rebuilding from the ground up?
  • How can we make use of the latest and greatest from A.I?
  • Can we continue to use our existing skills?
  • Can we remain compliant to our regulatory requirements?

The goal of this series of blogs is to outline what customers on BW 7.5 can do to modernise their mission critical system of engagement at their own pace whilst embracing A.I, ensuring they avoid expensive regret cost migrations to third party platforms that can often spiral, seldom delivering on the initial promise. These blogs are based on first hand experiences in a pilot project for BW7.5 to BDC.

Interested to learn more? Read on

The Problem Statement

BW 7.5 is the last version of the ABAP stack SAP Business Warehouse that was initially released in the late 1990’s, it delivers out of the box capabilities (business content, extractor logic, authorisation concepts etc) to manage complex third normal form data structures from SAP ECC (legacy SAP application around finance, HR, supply chain etc) across a layered scalable architecture (LSA). In 2016, the ‘new’ BW was released called BW/4HANA with a roadmap to 2040+, triggering a timeline that will culminate in 2027 with the end of mainstream maintenace of the ABAP version BW7.5.

However, many customers for a variety of reasons chose not to move to BW4. Either they have too much logic built in objects not easily ported (for example APDs, BEx) or they were enticed by promises made by best of breed data vendors in cloud native data lake architectures to migrate their BW workload. Often these project spiral in costs and effort without delivering sufficient value.

The Solution

SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC), released in February 2025 is SAP’s fully managed SaaS solution built for modern workloads and to natively handle data-as-a-service from across the SAP Business Suite applications (S/4HANA Public and Private, SuccessFactors, Concur etc). This is achieved at the click of a button whereby ‘Data Products’ are loaded and kept in sync with backend transactional systems, removing the leg work for customers looking to get rapid value from their data. Business Content and Intelligent applications are installed via the BDC Cockpit, surfacing KPIs for decision making across a host of business areas, with a vast amount more to come in 2026. With BDC, SAP Databricks, SAP Snowflake and other best of breed solutions work in tandem with SAP Datasphere on the semantically rich data products providing customers a unified data architecture to deliver next generation A.I use cases.

What adds to the excitement of BW customers setting their new year goals? BDC is the go-to solution for SAP Business Warehouse. BDC provides a strong roadmap for customers to modernise their BW7.5 environment at their own pace, retaining their investments, their skills, keeping their data available and opening their rich sets of historic business data for A.I. This is achieved through the ‘Lift, Shift & Innovate’ approach that allows for large complex BWs to move to a managed cloud service (Private Cloud Edition) of BW within the formation of SAP Business Data Cloud.

The Lift phase moves existing SAP BW systems as-is to SAP-managed cloud infrastructure, extending maintenance timelines (2030 instead of 2027) and reducing operational overhead. The Shift phase exposes SAP BW data as Data Products using the Data Product Generator tool, enabling integration with SAP Datasphere and external data sources while preserving existing use cases. The Innovate phase explores SAP-managed Data Products and Intelligent Applications to replace legacy SAP BW scenarios with modern analytics, AI capabilities, and access to an open partner ecosystem. This phased approach mitigates risk by maintaining existing investments while providing a clear path to cloud-native analytics and reduced Total Cost of Ownership.

There are some prerequisites for your BW system to fulfil prior to embarking on the journey with BDC, the minimum version supporting the data product generator is SPS 24 and it needs to be running on HANA.

Many large global organisations reliant on BW for their strategic reporting, planning/business steering have already begun the transition to BDC to realise the benefits of modernisation to cloud native at their own pace.

‘Vibe Migration’

In Autumn/Winter 2025 the SAP EMEA and Global Solution Advisory teams worked cohesively on a pilot to demonstrate the BW to BDC move for a specific customer case.

This case assessed all aspects of a complex BW7.5 system after the Lift to PCE, demonstrating how the Shift and Innovate phases can look using the modern tools available in SAP BDC with a focus on SAP Datasphere, SAP Analytics Cloud and Artificial Intelligence. Throughout the subsequent blog posts we will share the findings with specifics on how end of life (EoL) objects such as APDs, BEx and other scenarios can be covered either through automated migration to SAP BDC Datasphere, A.I assisted conversion or rebuild.

Please stay tuned for the next in this series of blog posts related to the Data Product Generator.

entry.title: Re: How to make Splited table by RAP?
entry.content: You can use Facets to create segments within a screen. By default facet is for object page. You can create as many facet as you want, it will show as tabbed view but all resides within same page. If you want to have the first page and multiple tables, then use Custom Page Fragment, which will give you full freedom to add as per your own choice, it will use standard RAP filter and List reporting feature as ususal.

entry.title: Re: How to disable the Manage My Substitution option in t...
entry.content:
Dear Customer,

In order to disable to Manage my My Substitution feature for end users within the My Inbox app, the Substitution parameter must be set to "false" as per the documentation below. By default it is set to "true"

Configure My Inbox Tile Parameters | SAP Help Portal

substitution

true|false

Enables or disables substitution feature.

This parameter also enables a column and filter option On Behalf Of in the Expert View of My Inbox.

Default value is true.

 

This is done via Custom Tiles:

Custom Tiles | SAP Help Portal

I hope this helps with your requirement.

Kind regards,

Ronan Cullen

entry.title: Design-First Event-Driven Architecture: Mastering Multi-Account AEM Management with SAP Event Portal
entry.content:
Join us for an insightful collaboration between Solace and SAP as we explore how to build robust, scalable event-driven architectures using a design-first approach with SAP Event Portal. This session will demonstrate how Event Portal empowers organizations to design, visualize, and govern their event-driven systems while seamlessly administering Advanced Event Mesh (AEM) brokers across multiple SAP BTP sub-accounts.

What You'll Learn

  • Design-First EDA Principles: Discover how a design-first approach accelerates development, improves collaboration, and ensures consistency across your event-driven architecture

  • Event Portal Capabilities: Learn how to leverage Event Portal to design event schemas, define application domains, and create a comprehensive catalog of your event-driven assets

  • Multi-Account AEM Management: Master the administration and lifecycle management of AEM brokers spanning multiple SAP BTP sub-accounts from a centralized control plane

  • Governance and Best Practices: Understand how to implement governance policies, maintain version control, and enforce standards across your distributed event mesh

entry.title: Re: BOM Approval - Public cloud
entry.content:
Good day @udara_9273 

Thank you for your question.

The public cloud handles your requirement using the standard ECM with Change Records and workflow-based approvals. So, your understanding is slightly off: ECM capabilities for BOM governance do exist in public cloud but they are done with released apps and SSCUIs and not with the classic on premise transactions.

See following references for more details:
https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/f369b2eff700401494ba6e7c9a573288/a22f954090ce41d28db23e6228020f94.html

help.sap.com/doc/ce01d82756b947a1a043a5d5a3204226/2602.00/en-US/WN_CE2602_EN.pdf 

The public cloud in that aspects allows you to do the following:

  • You can create and change BOMs through Engineering Change Record process
  • You can use workflow steps in the change process before release
  • Release the approved change so BOM is effectively controlled by revision logic.

Let me know if you have further questions.

 

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: SAP DM - Import /export user defined schemas in highe...
entry.content:
Hi Ankit,

I understand the frustration—recreating schemas manually is a recipe for typos and a major time sink. The good news is that you don't have to start from scratch.

In SAP Digital Manufacturing (DM), you should use the Manage Digital Manufacturing Content app to handle transport between environments. Instead of a traditional export/import button within the schema configuration itself, you create a Content Package in your Source (Dev) system, add your User Defined Schemas (under the Extensibility or Configuration categories), and then use the Transport feature to push them to your QA or PRD subaccounts. Ensure that your Destination settings in the SAP BTP Cockpit are correctly configured to link your DM instances, or you can download the package as a .zip file and manually upload it if a direct transport route isn't established.

entry.title: Re: SAP Cloud Connector - websocket connection in SAP clo...
entry.content:
Well, I think that 400~500 ms is already *very fast* in a landscape like this... (The bandwidth can usually be increased, but the latency is more or less a hard physical limit determined by the distance between two points. I wouldn't be surprised if most of those 400~500 ms is due to the network latency between on-premises location and the Cloud data center.)

This is probably the best you can expect when communicating "across the internet"... The only way to make it faster would be: don't communicate across the internet. (I.e. don't use Cloud. Put both components, SAP DM and MQTT, into the same LAN segment. According to What Are Good Latency & Ping Speeds? | PingPlotter it is not unusual to have a latency of 100ms on an average internet connection, depending on distance and the number of routers that can forward a TCP packet to the next hop only after they have completely received it. In a good Gigabit-LAN, the latency is 1ms, so it could well be that 200ms of your 400-500ms roundtrip time are due to just latency?!)

entry.title: Depreciation Posted Monthly Despite Planned Useful Life of 1 Period
entry.content:
Dear SAP Community, 

We are facing an issue with asset depreciation and would appreciate some guidance.

We have an asset that was created in March, with the capitalization date 31.03.2025. In transaction AS03, under Depreciation Area 01 (HB1), the following settings are maintained:

  • Depreciation Key: "Linear over remaining useful life, pro rata, down to zero"

  • Planned useful life in periods: 1

  • Depreciation calculation start date: 01.01.2025

Despite these settings, depreciation is being posted every month, which is not what we expect given the planned useful life of 1 period.

We initially tried changing the depreciation key to "100% depreciation" to see if this would resolve the issue — could this be a valid approach, or might it cause other inconsistencies?

Additionally, we are using transaction AFAR (Recalculate Depreciation):

  • How should AFAR be used correctly in this scenario?

  • Is it required after changing the depreciation key or useful life settings to ensure correct depreciation posting?

Any insights into the root cause or best practice for handling this would be greatly appreciated.

Thanks in advance!

entry.title: Re: Closing the order line has verified the material status.
entry.content: As I know, No. Closing an order item is treated as an item-level change. If the DChain-specific material status in Sales View 1 blocks order creation or changes, the check is triggered and the error is raised by design. The message cannot be removed; possibly, only material status customizing or a temporary status change can resolve it.

entry.title: Re: SPL City field is blank
entry.content:
The City field cannot be conditionally ignored when empty. If included in the SPL profile, an empty City is processed as empty and cannot be bypassed. 
Stopwords and field-specific match percentages do not apply to City. Matching thresholds are profile-wide.
To avoid missed hits, may exclude City from the SPL profile.

entry.title: Re: Issue with SAP HANA Express Edition License on AWS Cloud
entry.content:
When the license expires, HANA switches to Invalid License, even if it was deployed via AWS Marketplace. You must either install a valid commercial HANA license or migrate to a supported HANA offering (e.g. full SAP HANA license or SAP HANA Cloud).
As I know, AWS Marketplace does not provide license renewal for HANA Express.

entry.title: Re: What transaction code can I use to export a list of e...
entry.content:
MM60 (Material List) or SE16N on table MARA / MVKE.
Filter by Material Status (e.g. Z1, Z2, Z3) and export the result.

entry.title: Re: Performance - PPH_HANA_ACTIVE Table
entry.content:
I would not use PPH_HANA_ACTIVE for custom logic.
It is SAP-internal and intended only for SAP programs/transactions, as stated in the notes.
Adding packages or Z-objects is not supported (as I know) and may negatively impact performance.

entry.title: Re: Replacing Allocation Structure in a Work Order
entry.content: As far as I know, this is not possible. The allocation structure is derived from the settlement profile and cannot be changed at work order level via BAPI or BAdI. To use a different allocation structure, a different settlement profile (and therefore a different order type) is required.

entry.title: Re: Unable to access the VK11 'Create Condition Records f...
entry.content:
App IDs VK11, VK12 and VK13 are not valid in Public Cloud environment as they are replaced by apps Set Material Prices - Sales, Set Service Prices - Sales and Set Tax Rates - Sales.

Refer this documentation: App Documentation 

  • You can manage material prices, service prices, and tax rates for sales by using the Set Material Prices – Sales app, Set Service Prices – Sales app, and Set Tax Rates – Sales app respectively.
  • You can manage both material and service prices in one place with the Manage Prices - Sales app. This app enables you to maintain multiple prices at one time and display condition records for different condition types in a list.

entry.title: Re: Error: Failed to get transports for selected package...
entry.content:
This usually means CTS can’t return any transport request for that package.
Use a transportable package (not $TMP), ensure the package has a Transport Layer assigned (SE80/Package), and that your user has CTS authorizations / open modifiable request in that system. Then deploy again.

entry.title: Re: Export Vendor returns
entry.content:
This looks to be by design.
Vendor returns use the plant -> shipping data sales area maintained in SPRO, not the BP’s sales area. Only one sales area can be assigned to the plant.

To resolve this, you must either extend Export vendors to the plant’s sales area (VNS1/10/00) or separate plants / shipping points for domestic vs. export scenarios.

entry.title: Re: HANA System Database copy scenario
entry.content:
The system database does not require XS roles for recovery. xs_worker / xs_standby are tenant-related roles and are not mandatory in the target system for system DB recovery.

Recovery fails only when the target system lacks mandatory system roles (worker/standby), not XS roles.

entry.title: Re: Inquiry about how to use the EXECUTE_UPDATE method of...
entry.content:
EXECUTE_UPDATE does not guarantee atomic processing for large parameter tables. For mass data, implicit DB limits (array size, packet size, resources) can cause partial execution without an ABAP error.
Split the data into smaller batches and commit explicitly. There is no fixed recommended size; it depends on the DB and system resources.
SAP recommendation: use package-based processing (e.g. 500–5,000 rows) and handle errors per batch.

entry.title: SAP Analytics Cloud Planning with Live Snowflake Data
entry.content:
Introduction

In Q4 2025, we released live versions for planning models in SAP Analytics Cloud: 

  • In seamless planning, you can connect live to fact data in SAP Datasphere (blogpost)
  • In non-seamless planning scenarios, you can connect to the live SQL sources available in SAP Analytics Cloud. Up until now, Google BigQuery was the only available source (blogpost)

With QRC1 2026, Snowflake is added to the portfolio of live sources and with that, you can now reference Snowflake data for planning without replication!

 

How does it work?

In my blogpost on live versions from Google BigQuery, I demonstrated how to add live data to an existing model. So this time, we will take another approach and create a planning model from data.

  • Go to the modeler
  • Create model
  • Create model from data
  • Choose Snowflake. You can filter the list of connections to Data Storage “Cloud”
  • Choose a connection.
  • Choose a view
  • The model is created along the source view’s structures. You see the instant live connection in the data preview

Model creation from live data

You can now work with this model as you know from model’s without live versions. In the example below, we remove dimensions and measures that we do not need. We re-create one of the measures as a live calculation.

Calculations on live data

We can also leverage the data in data actions. See this example where we copy the Actual data into a plan version with a factor. The actual version that you see is sourced directly from Snowflake. When we copy the data into a plan version, the plan data is stored in the fact table of our model in SAP Analytics Cloud. 

Planning

 

FAQ

Where can I use this feature?

This feature is available for SAP Analytics Cloud models that are not deployed to SAP Datasphere (=not using seamless planning).

Can I use any source view?

Yes. However, not all data types are supported (details).

What can I do with live versions? (And what can I not do?)

  • You can display the live version data in the model data foundation and in tables, charts, etc. in stories.
  • You can reference the live version data in model and story calculations as well as data actions (incl. advanced formulas).
  • You cannot write back to the source. You can copy the live version data into planning versions via copy/paste in the table, data actions and version management.

How do I see if data is live or not?

  • In models, a live version is highlighted in the data preview. In the version dimension, you see the data source per version. 
  • In tables, live version data is locked for data entry and formatted accordingly. Reasons for unplannable data give accurate information.

 

Conclusion

Live data access in planning models represents a massive opportunity. SAP Analytics Cloud aligns with SAP Business Data Cloud’s ambition to be open and keeps on expanding its live connectivity portfolio. Look forward to use your Azure data for planning in the future and follow the respective innovation in the Roadmap Explorer.

entry.title: Re: I can't find SSCUI 102258 in the Central Business Con...
entry.content:
Dear @seonjoong,

In Public Cloud the activity you’re looking for is not Define Discounting Rules (ID 102258) anymore

For Discounting of Long-Term Assets and Liabilities in Advanced Valuation, SAP current configuration IDs are:

  • Define Discounting Rules -> Configuration Activity ID 104799
  • Assign Discounting Rules to Accounting Principle -> Configuration Activity ID 104800

SAP Note below:

https://me.sap.com/notes/0003216580

SAP explicitly lists these IDs in the official Note for this process, along with the prerequisite Advanced Valuation config steps for example Activate Advanced Valuation ->ID 103315

So, 102258 is effectively outdated for this topic either an older mapping, documentation artifact or it now refers to something else and you should use 104799/104800 instead.

Still above doesn’t appear, probably the 3 most common causes:

1. Advanced Valuation not activated in configuration sequence
Make sure Activate Advanced Valuation (103315) was executed SAP lists this as step 1 prerequisite

2. Scope item mismatch
SAP references the process under Accounting and Financial Close (J58) in the official guidance. Ensure your activated scope covers this content

3. Missing configuration authorizations in CBC for example role

Best Regards,

Dawid

Also it isn't standart so you need to take care of it. hybris/spartacus releases new features and bugfixes. You can easy upgrade it.
